>the law of supply and demand doesn’t apply to Monero if people like it and want to hold it
I’m just so tired of shills/retards/BTC-brainbroken people applying new rules just for Monero. What does it even mean for a coin to be “primarily a SoV”? People use Monero for whatever reason they want. Some use it primarily for transacting (because it works) and some use it primarily for holding (because it works). That’s how a fucking functional currency operates.

>>62471322

At the end of the day it comes down to whether you actually believe crypto is a real technology or not. Is it possible to create a censorship resistant asset with privacy that actually works as the native internet currency of the world? Because Monero is LITERALLY the only one that even has a chance of becoming that—-the entire point of crypto in the first place. No amount of regulation or liquidity restriction can prevent it from taking hold eventually. The world is far too multipolar now, no single nation can stop it.

62473396
>>62473376
I already established that if XMR was primarily used as an sov it's demand would drop massively. Fomo only works if the product is attractive due to internal motivation

62473588
>>62473396
What the fuck do you mean you “established it”? You literally just asserted it without the slightest attempt at supporting your argument.

“Monero demand would not drop massively if people held it. In fact that claim violates the most basic principles of economics”

There. I established the counterpoint. What now?
>(am I actually just arguing with an autistic teenager? It seems like it)

62473623
>>62473588
>What the fuck do you mean you “established it”? You literally just asserted it without the slightest attempt at supporting your argument.
The word 'if' signals that an argument makes an assumption. Any discussions derived from that argument which are not attacking the assumption itself presuppose the assumption.
>There. I established the counterpoint.
Your point isn't countering mine. That's because I used the word 'primarily'. This means the assumption of my argument is that over 50% of Monero's 'usage' was solely as an sov. Your point is about any non-zero amount of XMR as an sov.
>In fact that claim violates the most basic principles of economics”
This is wrong. There is simply no reason to buy your bags. If Monero was primarily used as an sov there would be no meaningful difference to a non-KYCed btc wallet. You could argue about fungibility now but not even half the board knows this term so this is a negligible factor.
I also told you before that even if you are about to argue that it worked out for btc you're neglecting
A. btc has now been adopted by the jewish system, Monero is too democratic in its power concentration to have the same happening to it
B. XMR's qualities stem from its transactional qualities so with every hodler added the appeal of XMR shrinks
So if you want to defend your position you now need to show us why people would be interested into entering XMR if it was *primarily* used as an sov. No one is arguing about supply and demand not existing as a framework for finding the value of something, we're arguing about demand existing in actuality under the assumption of XMR mainly being used as an sov.

62474147
>>62473623
>Any discussions derived from that argument which are not attacking the assumption itself presuppose the assumption.
This statement alone should be enough to stop anyone from ever talking to you again.

62474174
>>62468583
correct. xmr is the real bitcoin because it has all the satoshi requirements

62474407
>>62473623
>I used the word 'primarily'
So? This whole idea that Monero is “primarily” any particular thing already presupposes your bullshit idea that MoE and SoV are inherently contradictory or adversarial with one another. They’re not.

Monero is not primarily anything. It’s not primarily transactional it’s not primarily store of value. It is whatever people want it to be.

>There is simply no reason to buy your bags.
lol ok weirdo I’m gonna just buy and hold Monero and nobody will ever be able to detect it, tax it, freeze it, trace it, or seize it. But apparently that’s a worthless utility.

>XMR's qualities stem from its transactional qualities
Repeating this doesn’t make it true btw.

62477241
>>62474147
Sadly it doesn't.

>>62474407
>This whole idea that Monero is “primarily” any particular thing already presupposes your bullshit idea that MoE and SoV are inherently contradictory or adversarial with one another.
No, I said that because you complained in >>62470104 about different people using XMR differently.
>They’re not.
The value proposition of XMR as an sov derives solely from its qualities of it being an moe. So by you arguing for sov, you're reducing its sov properties.
>It is whatever people want it to be.
But then he cries about >>62468595
>But apparently that’s a worthless utility.
It's only not worthless because people actually use it.
>Repeating this doesn’t make it true btw.
It being true is what makes it true.

62477452
>>62477241
Why wouldn't someone just use the xmr to swap for btc and retain the same level of privacy until they're ready to sell? Fungibility? If that was such a significant issue then there would be absolutely no offers to buy btc on haveno. Monero will always be a useful privacy layer for crypto but it can hardly be considered a hedge against btc so what's the upside?

62477516
>>62477452
A black box system can easily be found out if you can closely watch the input and output. This means you're basically just creating a convoluted btc mixer, even if you use different wallets.

62477900
>>62477516
But holding xmr is essentially holding the black box so if the sov prop of xmr still stands then it doesn't matter whether having btc associated with xmr is a problem. Mixers are only flawed by being transparent and xmr fixes this so the glorified btc mixer will work as intended. Input/output correlation could be feasible when making transfers over a short timeframe for the same amounts but, assuming the new non-kyc btc bought with xmr is spent on a non-personally identifiable ip address, I can't see how holding and swapping back to xmr would be disadvantageous compared to just holding xmr. This is also assuming that btc does manage to hold it's value for the next few decades and hasn't become unusable/insecure/inflationary in the meantime

62475644
>>62475427
>>62475451
The thing about AI is it is useful, but even in its current iteration it has fundamental flaws that mean for specific jobs it can never do as good as an experienced human. Inexperienced people using AI will do better than if they didn't in many cases, but anybody with decent experience sees the output and sees all of the things wrong with it.
As someone put it, people think AI makes good charts or slides or reports, and then the people who are really good at making charts or slides or reports all say "No that looks bad".

I'm a software engineer and I use a decent amount of AI and had it do great for me, but there's still a number of problems I've thrown at it and it produced total unworkable trash. The thing is, if I was inexperienced I would think it looked good. As someone with 15 years of experience though, I could see its solution would break down rapidly with any other alterations we needed to make.
The experienced human in the loop is still required for anything beyond the absolute basic. As someone who is also in the financial space, AI is absolutely not going to be capable of keeping all the ducks of accounting and finance laws in a row versus an experienced human being.

even though I'm not a neet after being laid off I feel like I'm in a similar situation.
I have a leg up with my work experience and education but I don't feel like it was worth it which meant that I wasted my youth doing something even worse than just fucking around

62474795
to all the people in this situation who want actual sane advice, here it:

Do something easy. Something that won't demand a superior work ethic or a miserable grind with a manager looking at production numbers watching you.
You're likely gonna hate your life every single morning you wake up and have to work AND you didn't do this when you were 20 years old and it was easy. You can get over the hump and become a "worker" but the mornings are already your enemy, don't stack the odds further against you.

Work behind a register. Ideally at a gas station or some sleepy business. Maybe a long shot for you to get hired, but it's ideal. Some cleaning, some dealing with dickheads, some stocking. You won't be worked to the bone. A grocery/dollar store cashier is worse in most cases.

Inbound call centre. Yes you will hate your job but the work comes at you and your office should be air conditioned. If you have superior (compared to your coworkers) manners and people skills you may even find yourself promoted rapidly. 60 year old grannies get jobs in call centers and end up trainers and managers, you can do it.

Janitor. This one may be a stretch for a 40yo NEET but if you get the right gig, or hop into the right gig after getting a year experience, you may have found a cozy job. Many janitors are not running around like rats and barely have to deal with coworkers or the public. Maybe you end up hired by the local school board in half a year and make "decent" money.


If you really want to try your hand at job where you have a taskmaster watching you try these:

Warehouse worker.
Meatpacker.
Day laborer: Construction, landscaping, whatever.
Factory as a production worker. GL getting hired unless the job is literal hell with high turnover.
Outbound Call Centre

Good luck. If you show up every day for a 1 year or 2 a manager or senior coworker will likely give you a glowing reference. You can even become a real "worker" and have easier mornings in time.

62474821
>>62474795
None of those are hiring.

Explain EVERYTHING. Learning how to utilize LLMs will really help you anyway. I'm getting close to 40 myself and I use LLMs daily (I am a professional with a good job not a NEET but it doesn't matter you need to use LLMs now even if you're employed). Anons ITT are mostly going to dunk on you because you're a huge fucking loser but the best time to turn your life around is right the fuck now. The LLM won't make you feel bad like everyone here is doing too.

Explain your interests to it too and maybe it can try and recommend a job you'll actually excel somewhat in, although beggars can't be choosy at this point.

>Well, I guess I've got nothing better to do.
Repeating myself, but the modern human needs to get comfortable using LLMs to be productive and successful. Avoid falling in to emotional or political traps involving them. View them as a useful tool. A human who refuses to use a calculator would be less productive than a human who does use a calculator.

The same is true for LLMs. Don't blindly trust everything it tells you. Get a feel for how to prompt it to give you the results you want and double check things that you aren't sure about. A lot of people on /biz/ and in general seethe over LLMs, call you a pajeet if you mention them etc, just ignore them. I'm a professional making 6 figs a year in an white collar position, I use LLMs every single day outside of work and inside of work.

As a skill-less NEET who doesn't know how to navigate the world it's the most invaluable tool at your disposal.

62477487
>>62477445
If you think those are the same thing I can see why you are frustrated with someone pointing out the error to begin with.

The market decides the price. Miners have 2 options:

1. Sell now to cover costs
2. Eat the costs and wait for the market to improve

Neither scenario is them setting the price. Scenario 2 is miners speculating with coins the same way any market participant does, except their tokens were previously uncirculating.
